sections.heading: Definition and Anatomy
sections.content: Esophageal cancer refers to malignant neoplasms arising from the esophagus, a muscular tube connecting the pharynx to the stomach. Embryologically, the esophagus derives from the foregut, with its upper third originating from the pharyngeal gut and the lower two-thirds from the embryonic esophagus. Anatomically, the esophagus extends from the cricopharyngeus muscle to the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ), which is defined as the point where the tubular esophagus meets the saccular stomach, often marked by the squamocolumnar junction (SCJ) or Z-line.

sections.heading: Histology and Barrett's Esophagus
sections.content: The esophagus is lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, which can undergo metaplasia to columnar epithelium (Barrett's esophagus), a precursor to adenocarcinoma.

sections.heading: Siewert Classification
sections.content: The esophagogastric junction (EGJ) is the junction between the esophagus and stomach, with tumors involving this area classified based on their epicenter relative to the GEJ. For adenocarcinomas, the Siewert classification categorizes EGJ tumors: Type I (1-5 cm above GEJ), Type II (true cardia, within 1 cm above and 2 cm below GEJ), and Type III (2-5 cm below GEJ, infiltrating upward).

sections.heading: TNM Staging
sections.content: Staging utilizes the TNM system, with T categories reflecting depth of invasion from mucosa (T1) to adventitia (T3) and adjacent structures (T4) [10, 73].
